bibliographic_data.title: Lessons Learned from Developing a Microservice Based Mobile Location-Based Crowdsourcing Platform
bibliographic_data.abstract: Research in Mobile Location-Based Crowdsourcing is hindered by a marked lack of real-world data. The development of a standardized, lightweight, easily deployable, modular, composable, and most of all, scalable experimentation framework would go a long way in facilitating such research. Conveniently, these are all salient characteristics of systems developed using a microservices approach. We propose QRowdsource - a MLBC experimentation framework built using a distributed services architecture. In this paper, we discuss the design and development of QRowdsource, from the decomposition of functional components to the orchestration of services within the framework. We also take a look at how the advantages and disadvantages of using a microservices approach translate to our specific use case and deliberate over a number of lessons learned while developing the experimentation framework.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes QRowdsource, a microservice-based platform for mobile location-based crowdsourcing (MLBC) that addresses the lack of real-world data in this field.
core_content.key_methodology: QRowdsource is built using a distributed services architecture and leverages QR-codes as location markers. The platform includes a Progressive Web Application (PWA) front-end, which allows users to scan QR-codes, complete tasks, and earn credits that can be redeemed for rewards.
outcomes: Developed QRowdsource, an MLBC platform using microservices architecture.
Implemented user authentication and task-based reward system.
Demonstrated the advantages of a microservice approach in developing scalable and composable systems.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper discusses the development of QRowdsource, a microservice-based platform for mobile location-based crowdsourcing (MLBC). The authors highlight the lack of real-world data in this field and propose using a microservices architecture to facilitate research. QRowdsource leverages QR-codes as location markers and includes a Progressive Web Application (PWA) front-end that allows users to scan QR-codes, complete tasks, and earn credits redeemable for rewards. The paper also delves into the advantages of microservices, such as independence, ease of testing, short redeployment times, high composability, and efficient scaling. Lessons learned from developing QRowdsource are discussed, including specific challenges and benefits in implementing a microservice architecture.
